title: SEGMENT 9 — Glow sticks you can turn on with a flashlight

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Glow sticks you can turn on with a flashlight
I want to introduce one more family of fluorescent proteins before our break, because they're conceptually different from everything we've covered so far.
So far, every protein we've discussed has been a single, fixed state — it folds, it matures, it glows, end of story.
You can't really turn it on or off.
But there's a category of fluorescent proteins that exist in multiple stable states and that can be deliberately switched between those states by light.
The analogy is: imagine a glow stick that's normally dark, but if you shine a flashlight on it, it turns on.
Now imagine that on a microscope you can shine that flashlight on exactly one tiny region of one cell — and suddenly only the molecules in that region are glowing, while everything else stays dark.
You've labeled a precise location in space.
Once you have that capability, you can do experiments that were previously impossible.
Let me walk through the categories.
Photoactivatable fluorescent proteins start dark and become bright after a brief pulse of activating light, usually in the ultraviolet or violet.
The prototype is PA-GFP, "photoactivatable GFP," from Jennifer Lippincott-Schwartz's lab in 2002.

PA-GFP is engineered such that its chromophore sits in a dim, neutral state until you hit it with a brief pulse of 405-nanometer light, which converts it to a bright state with about a hundred-fold increase in fluorescence.
So: take a cell expressing PA-GFP uniformly throughout the cytoplasm.
Shine 405 light at a tiny spot — say, near the nucleus.
Suddenly, only the molecules right there light up.
Now you can watch where they diffuse to over the next seconds and minutes.
You've created a glowing population in one location and you can follow it through the cell.
This is called photolabeling, and it's a way of asking "where do these molecules go?
" with spatial precision.
Photoswitchable proteins can be switched on and off, reversibly.
A classic example is Dronpa, from a coral.
Dronpa starts in a bright green state.
A pulse of blue light slowly switches it to a dark state.
A pulse of violet light switches it back to bright.
So you have a reversible on-off switch, controllable by light.
This will turn out to matter for super-resolution.
Photoconvertible proteins — and these are perhaps the most useful — start in one color and can be permanently switched to a different color by a pulse of light.
The leading examples are Dendra2 and mEos.
Both start green.
Hit them with a pulse of 405-nanometer light, and a specific peptide bond breaks inside the chromophore.
This breaks open a part of the structure, extends the conjugated electronic system, and shifts the emission permanently from green to red.
Think about what that lets you do.

You can take a cell expressing mEos all over, hit just one organelle — say, the Golgi apparatus — with a pulse of violet light, and now your Golgi-resident protein is red while the rest of the protein in the cell is still green.
Then you can watch the red signal flow out of the Golgi to wherever the protein is being trafficked.
The fluorescent tag has become a clock and a tracker at the same time.
This is a powerful experimental capability.
Why do these matter?
Three reasons, and they preview things we'll come back to.
First, spatial pulse-chase.
You can mark molecules in one place and watch where they go.
This is huge for studying trafficking, diffusion, and protein dynamics.
Second, super-resolution.
Conventional fluorescence microscopy can't resolve features smaller than about two hundred nanometers — that's the famous diffraction limit.

But if you can switch individual molecules on and off, one at a time, you can localize each one with much higher precision and accumulate them into an image that's much sharper than diffraction theory says is possible.
This is the trick behind PALM and STORM, which we'll cover in Segment 13.
Photoactivatable proteins were the enabling tools.
Third, all-optical control.
Once you can control protein states with light, you can begin to engineer optical switches into many parts of biology — not just for imaging, but for active perturbation.
This connects to optogenetics, which we'll cover in Segment 17.
This is a good place for our break.

So far we've covered: what fluorescent proteins are, the jellyfish story, Chalfie's worm, the structure of GFP, color engineering, the red proteins from coral, the mFruits and why monomeric matters, the rubric for choosing among them, and the photoactivatable variants.

In the second half, we'll cover sensors — how to turn fluorescent proteins from passive markers into active measurement devices — and we'll cover the microscopes you use to image them, including the super-resolution methods that broke the diffraction limit.
Then we'll talk about complementary labeling tools, putting fluorescent proteins into living tissue, and the related world of optogenetics.
Stretch your legs, get some water, and I'll see you in a few minutes.
